steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Standard English Conventions / Boundaries question. The sentence reads: “the most prestigious ______ the Nobel Prize in Literature.” “The Nobel Prize in Literature” renames and specifies “the most prestigious award for a person of letters” — it is an appositive. A colon is the correct punctuation to introduce an appositive or explanatory phrase that follows a complete noun phrase.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — award; for a person of letters,: A semicolon must join two independent clauses. “Award” alone is not an independent clause, and splitting “for a person of letters” away from “award” creates an ungrammatical fragment on each side. Semicolon rule violated. ✗
  • Choice B — award for a person of letters;: Places a semicolon before “the Nobel Prize in Literature,” which is a noun phrase, not an independent clause. A semicolon cannot introduce a noun phrase appositive. Semicolon rule violated. ✗
  • Choice C — award: for a person of letters: Places the colon after “award,” cutting “for a person of letters” off from “award” and making it the item being introduced by the colon. But “for a person of letters” is a modifier of “award,” not the appositive — the appositive is “the Nobel Prize in Literature.” Misplaced colon. ✗
  • Choice D — award for a person of letters:: Keeps the full noun phrase “the most prestigious award for a person of letters” intact, then uses a colon to introduce the appositive “the Nobel Prize in Literature.” Correct colon usage. ✓
That is the correct answer.
